<!-- Generated by a research-backed multi-agent workflow on 2026-06-28. Sources cited inline as [shorthand]. -->

# Nudo Journal — Customization & Feature Design

**For:** Founder + Engineer
**Scope:** Make the Nudo Journal more customizable for a BEGINNER → ENTHUSIAST audience across all home brew methods — without turning it into an intimidating pro tool.
**Codebase grounding (verified):** Next.js 14 (App Router) + Supabase (Postgres + RLS, `auth.uid() = user_id`), server actions for writes. Schema in `supabase/migrations/0001_journal.sql`; types in `types/database.ts`; writes in `lib/journal/actions.ts`; reads in `lib/journal/queries.ts`; method metadata in `lib/journal/methods.ts`; the single flat form in `components/journal/BrewForm.tsx`; the method selector in `components/journal/MethodPicker.tsx`.

---

## Executive summary

- **The journal today is one flat form that shows the same six fields for every method** (`BrewForm.tsx`: grind / dose / yield / time / temp / gear), a 1–5 star rating, and a single freeform `gear_used` box. Great *beginner* product, dead-end *enthusiast* product: a French press is asked for "Yield" and "Time" it doesn't need, while pour-over has nowhere to put bloom or pours. We fix this with **progressive depth**, not more always-on fields.
- **Architecture is settled and migration-safe:** keep the proven flat columns exactly as they are; add depth two ways — (a) new RLS'd reusable entities (`gear`, `recipes`, `water_profiles`) and (b) **one validated JSONB `details` column** on `brews` for method-specific/advanced/custom values. Drive the form from an extended `lib/journal/methods.ts` config. A 50-brew beginner who buys a Niche Zero hits **no migration, no re-keying** — old brews keep their meaning, new fields simply appear.
- **The single biggest NOW item is bigger than it looks.** `MethodPicker` is *not* controlled — it holds its own `useState('picked')` and exposes only a hidden input (verified, lines 13/20), so `BrewForm` cannot see the chosen method at render time. Per-method dynamic fields require a real **controlled-component refactor first**, then incremental per-method config. We split that work in two.
- **Three corrections to the draft that protect this brand specifically:** (1) the "change one thing" mechanic must be reachable on a **first-ever brew** (cold start), not only when pre-filling, or the brand's signature loop is invisible to the exact "first brews" user Nudo targets; (2) a missing **edit-brew** action is a true quick win and a prerequisite for trusting richer data — pulled into NOW; (3) **seeded starter recipes + an in-form timer** are more on-brand and higher-leverage for beginners than custom fields/reorderable fields, so those enthusiast-skewed items are demoted to LATER.
- **Hard caps make simplicity enforceable, not aspirational:** the Simple form is capped at **five stable, visually-consistent slots** that *relabel* (never add/remove cards) when the method changes; nothing always-on is ever added to Simple; the happy path is fully usable with only bean + dose + water/yield + time + rating.

---

## Design principles

> **Simple by default. Opt-in depth. Change one thing — from the very first brew.**

1. **Simple by default — and *stable*.** The Simple form is **five fixed slots**: `Dose` · `Water-or-Yield` · `Time-or-Steep` · `Grind` · `Taste`. When the method changes, slots **relabel** ("Yield" → "Water", "Time" → "Steep time"); the screen does **not** add or remove cards. This corrects the draft's "fewer fields is pure win" framing — for a 20s–40s entry user, a form that *changes shape* between sessions reads as "unpredictable," not "helpful." Consistency is a feature [mooremomentum minimal-habit-tracking].
2. **Progressive disclosure, remembered per method-family.** Depth is revealed conditionally (Nielsen's patterns: step-by-step, conditional toggle, contextual reveal) [uxpin; ixdf] — by (a) flipping **Advanced**, (b) gear that justifies it (a stepless grinder unlocks the dial widget), or (c) method that justifies it (espresso reveals pressure; pour-over reveals bloom). The Advanced preference is stored **per method-family, not globally**, so a user can have deep espresso and dead-simple French press at once — which is exactly the brand's "grow per method" premise.
3. **Change one thing — the spine, from brew #1.** "Repeat last brew, change one thing" is the primary returning action, and `variable_changed` graduates from freeform text to a *structured* field [bilgebrew change-one-variable; perfectdailygrind]. Critically, a first-ever brew (no prior to repeat) still **teaches** the mechanic via a one-line prompt, so Nudo's defining idea isn't invisible to first-brewers.
4. **No graduation wall.** New depth layers *alongside* the flat columns (entities + one JSONB column). `on delete set null`, never cascade-clobber. Old `grind_clicks`/`gear_used` keep meaning forever.
5. **Auto-derive, don't ask.** Ratio from dose+water/yield; extraction-quality label from ratio/time; days-off-roast from `roast_date` (already in `freshness.ts`) [features; coffeeadastra].

---

## Architecture decisions the whole roadmap depends on

### A. Keep the flat columns; add depth in two ways
The existing `brews` columns stay **exactly** as they are — they map to the universal core every method shares and to what `BrewForm` and the dashboard already read. Depth is added via:
- **New related entities** (`gear`, `recipes`, `water_profiles`) — first-class, RLS'd, reusable across brews (the proven Beanconqueror / Timer.Coffee / Notion shape) [beanconqueror; notion templates].
- **One JSONB column** `brews.details jsonb not null default '{}'` for method-specific / advanced / custom values (bloom, pours, pressure, puck prep, TDS/EY, taste axes, custom-field values, structured `change`).

**Why JSONB, not 30 nullable columns:** the field set must be *per-method* and *user-extensible*. Modelling that as columns means a wide, mostly-null table and a migration per method field. JSONB lets the **form config (TypeScript) own the schema** while Postgres stores it cheaply. We still **promote** a value to a real column the moment we want to chart/filter on it cross-method (the way `dose_g` already is).

> Engineer notes: (1) GIN index only if/when we query *inside* `details`; for now it's write-and-read-by-id, so a plain column suffices. (2) **Validate `details` server-side** against the active method's field config before insert — coerce by `kind`, drop unknown keys, clamp per-family ranges; never trust the client payload.

### B. The "form config" is the heart of customization
`lib/journal/methods.ts` already holds per-method metadata (`defaultDoseRange`, `defaultRatio`, `emoji`, `short`). Extend it into the **single source of truth for which fields each method shows, at which depth, in what order.** `BrewForm` becomes a renderer driven by this config.

```ts
// lib/journal/methods.ts — extended
type FieldDepth = "core" | "advanced";
type FieldKind  = "number" | "text" | "grind" | "ratio" | "bloom"
                | "pours" | "select" | "slider" | "pressure" | "timer";
type CoreSlot   = "dose" | "liquid" | "time" | "grind" | "taste"; // the 5 stable slots

type MethodField = {
  key: string;            // form name; maps to a brews column OR details.<key>
  label: string;          // slot RELABEL (e.g. "Water" vs "Yield")
  kind: FieldKind;
  depth: FieldDepth;      // core = one of the 5 stable slots; advanced = behind toggle
  slot?: CoreSlot;        // which fixed slot a core field occupies (enforces the cap)
  storeAs: "column" | "details";
  unit?: "g" | "s" | "temp" | "bar" | "%";
  hint?: string;          // 1-line, friendly, no-gatekeeping
};

type MethodFamily = "espresso" | "pour-over" | "immersion" | "stovetop" | "drip-bag";
// each METHODS entry gains: family + fields: MethodField[]
```

This is the lever the research keeps pointing at: *"Make the brew form driven by Preparation Method... this single decision separates the praised apps from the 'too overwhelming' or 'too rigid' ones"* [beanconqueror implications]. The `slot` field is what enforces Principle 1's five-slot cap mechanically — a reviewer can reject any config that puts two core fields in the same slot or invents a sixth.

### C. The MethodPicker refactor is the real long pole (not the config)
`MethodPicker` is **uncontrolled** (verified): internal `useState('picked')`, output via a hidden `<input>`. For fields to re-render on method change, it must become **controlled** (`value` + `onChange` lifted into `BrewForm`), or `BrewForm` must own method state and pass it down. This touches a shared component and is the actual bulk of N1's effort — not the "mechanical renderer swap" framing.

---

## Roadmap

Effort: **S** ≈ 1–2 days · **M** ≈ 3–5 days · **L** ≈ 1–2 weeks.

### TIER 1 — NOW (quick wins, high leverage, low risk)

| ID | Feature | Effort | New table? | Touches |
|----|---------|--------|-----------|---------|
| N0 | **Edit-brew action** (`updateBrew`) | S | no | actions.ts, brew detail/edit page |
| N1a | **Controlled-method refactor** + `details` column + server-validation scaffold | M | no (JSONB add) | MethodPicker, BrewForm, actions, types, `0002` |
| N1b | **Per-method core/advanced field config** (espresso first, incremental) | M | no | methods.ts, field handlers |
| N2 | **Advanced toggle** (per method-family, remembered) | S | no | BrewForm, profiles pref |
| N3 | **Structured "change one thing"** (+ cold-start teaching) | S–M | no | BrewForm, actions, `details.change` |
| N4 | **"Repeat last brew" as primary action** | S | no | dashboard, new-brew page, queries |
| N5 | **Profile preferences** (units, defaults, per-family mode) | S | no (cols on profiles) | profiles, queries, actions, units.ts, `0003` |
| N6 | **Seeded starter recipes** (static, beginner half of recipes) | S | no (static) | recipes.ts, BrewForm empty state |

**N0. Edit-brew action.** Today there is `createBrew` and `deleteBrew` but **no `updateBrew`** (verified) — beginners who fat-finger dose/time must delete and re-log. Adding richer per-brew data makes a missing edit path worse. *Why:* forgiveness matters more to beginners than to experts; trustworthy data needs a fix path. *Schema mapping:* add `updateBrew(id, formData)` mirroring `updateBean`'s shape (same `requireUserId`, same coercion helpers); new `app/account/journal/[id]/edit/page.tsx` reusing `BrewForm` with `prefill`. No schema change. **Effort S.**

**N1a. Controlled-method refactor + `details` + validation scaffold.** *Why:* the same field name means different things across families — espresso "time" ≈ 25–35s vs filter ≈ 3–6min; espresso ratio ≈ 1:2 vs filter ≈ 1:16 — and brewer geometry "systematically shifts which variables dominate" [variables-per-method implications; coffeeadastra; podiumcoffeeclub; kurasu]. *Schema mapping:* `0002_brew_details.sql`: `alter table public.brews add column details jsonb not null default '{}'::jsonb;` (RLS is row-level — no policy change). Refactor `MethodPicker` to controlled (`value`/`onChange`); lift `method` into `BrewForm` state so the field list re-renders on change. Add `details: Json` to `Brew`/`BrewInsert`. In `createBrew`/`updateBrew`, resolve the method's field config, write column-backed fields as today, assemble `details` from `storeAs:"details"` fields, **coercing/validating by `kind` and rejecting unknown keys** (today's validation is just `num`/`int`/`str`, lines 18–36, so this is a new per-kind layer). **Effort M.**

**N1b. Per-method field config (incremental).** Populate `fields` per family, espresso first, the way the draft already proposes shipping families one at a time. *Beginner:* core stays the **five stable slots, relabeled** per method (see Deep-dive 2). *Enthusiast:* Advanced reveals family-appropriate depth. **Effort M.**

**N2. Advanced toggle — per method-family.** A single switch: **"Simple · Advanced"**, default Simple, revealing `depth:"advanced"` fields inline under a calm "More detail — optional" divider. *Correction vs draft:* the preference is stored **per family** (`profiles.brew_detail jsonb`, e.g. `{"espresso":"advanced","french-press":"simple"}`), not one global flag — so deep espresso and simple French press coexist, and a global "always advanced" can't trap a beginner on a shared device. *Why:* progressive disclosure is the canonical "simple by default, powerful when needed" pattern; over-tracking drives abandonment, so depth must be opt-in [uxpin; logrocket; mooremomentum]. *Schema mapping:* UI state seeded from N5 preference. **Effort S.** *This is the protection mechanism every heavy feature hides behind.*

**N3. Structured "change one thing" — incl. cold start.** Replace the freeform `variable_changed` input with a chip picker: `Grind · Dose · Water/ratio · Temp · Time · Technique · Bean · Nothing`; tapping `Grind` reveals a finer/coarser toggle; free-text remains as "something else." *Cold-start fix (the draft's gap):* the card today renders **only when `prefill` is set** (verified, line 85), so a first-ever brew never sees the mechanic. On a first brew we instead show a one-line, no-input teaser — *"Nudo's one rule: next time, change just one thing. We'll help you track it."* — so the brand idea lands on brew #1. *Enthusiast:* on repeat, the chip auto-fills from a **diff** (dose 18→18.5, grind 12→11) and nudges "pick the one that mattered," storing `{field,direction,from,to}`. *Schema mapping:* keep `brews.variable_changed text` (human summary, read by the detail page); add `details.change`. New `ChangeOneThing` component writes both. **Effort S–M.**

**N4. "Repeat last brew" as primary action.** Promote pre-fill into a first-class **"Brew this again →"** button on the dashboard recent list, brew detail, and bean page — one tap clones the last brew into a draft with the change-one-thing chips open. *Why:* sub-few-seconds entry is the top predictor of whether logging sticks [mooremomentum; progression; presscoffee]. *Schema mapping:* no schema change. `getLastBrewForBean` exists; add `getLastBrew()` and a `?from=<brewId>` clone path on `app/account/journal/new/page.tsx` (which already supports `?bean=`). **Effort S.**

**N5. Profile preferences (units, defaults, per-family mode).** Use the barely-used `profiles` table for the handful of settings that personalize the app. *Beginner:* defaults are good enough that they never open Settings; US users see °F/oz without touching anything [scales implications]. *Enthusiast:* a small `/account/settings` panel. *Why:* sensible defaults + remembered mode are the highest-leverage retention moves; "users rarely change settings" [useronboard]. See Deep-dive 4 for the schema and the **rounding-safety fix** the draft waved away. **Effort S** (storage; onboarding screen is LATER).

**N6. Seeded starter recipes (the empty state is the front door).** For a *"For every first brews"* brand, the empty state **is** the product. Ship canonical recipes as **static data** — "Hoffmann V60," "AeroPress (forgiving)," "Modern espresso 1:2," "Hoffmann French press." Empty state offers them; tap → form pre-filled → brew → save. *Why:* seeded content beats blank forms; this answers "what now?" for the literal target user who has no prior brew [useronboard; smashingmag empty-state]. *Schema mapping:* `lib/journal/recipes.ts` (no DB). The user-saved recipes **table** stays in NEXT (X6). **Effort S.** *(Pulled forward from the draft's NEXT.)*

---

### TIER 2 — NEXT (the depth layer; the beginner→enthusiast bridge)

| ID | Feature | Effort | New table? | Touches |
|----|---------|--------|-----------|---------|
| X1 | **Gear entities** (replace freeform `gear_used`) | M | **gear** | actions, BrewForm, GearForm/Picker, `0004` |
| X2 | **Gear library presets** | S | no (static) | gearLibrary.ts |
| X3 | **Per-grinder grind widget** (gated on X1) | M | no | GrindField, gear.spec, `details.grind` |
| X4 | **Method-family advanced fields** | M–L | no | methods.ts, field handlers, details |
| X5 | **Structured tasting** (behind "Add detail") | M | no | TasteField, `details.taste` |
| X6 | **User-saved recipes** (table; keeper + per-bean baseline) | M | **recipes** (+`keeper` col) | recipes.ts, BrewForm, `0005` |
| X7 | **In-form brew timer** | S–M | no | TimerField, `details` timestamps |
| X8 | **Water tracking** (categorical default, chemistry on demand) | M | **water_profiles** | WaterField, `0006` |
| X9 | **Brew photo + bag-depletion** (beginner delight) | S–M | no | brews.photo_url, bean dose-sum query |

**X1. Gear entities.** See Deep-dive 1. *Why:* "make gear first-class, reusable entities, not free text... so future analytics are possible," and the grinder must be an entity because *the same grind number means different things on different grinders* [scales implications; grinder implications; honestcoffeeguide]. **Effort M.**

**X2. Gear library presets.** Static catalog of ~20 popular grinders + common brewers; user *selects* rather than types specs, with a Custom fallback. *Why:* "ship a built-in grinder library so beginners select once and get the correct widget and sensible ranges" — the strongest beginner-onboarding feature observed [grinder implications; features implications; single origin; dial in coffee]. *Schema mapping:* `lib/journal/gearLibrary.ts` (no table); picking copies `spec` into the `gear` row and sets `library_id`. **Effort S.**

**X3. Per-grinder grind widget.** The grind input adapts to the selected grinder's adjustment type (stepper / rotations+clicks / sub-click decimal / stepless free). *Correction vs draft:* this is **gated on X1** and the controlled-method refactor, and makes the new-brew page a heavier fetch (beans + gear + library spec + prefill) — not independent. **For pre-ground/fixed-grinder beginners, "pre-ground (can't change)" is a first-class grind option, and the dial-in coach must never tell them to grind finer.** *Why:* "do NOT store grind as a single free-text field; support multiple entry modes driven by the grinder; for stepless owners the app IS the missing memory"; µm math is non-linear (Encore ESP 20µm then 90µm) so microns are Advanced-only and never `setting × constant` [grinder implications]. *Schema mapping:* `details.grind = {grinder_id, mode, value, rotations?, clicks?, microns?}`; keep `grind_clicks text` as the human label. **Effort M.**

**X4. Method-family advanced fields.** Fill the `depth:"advanced"` sets per family (all behind N2): espresso (temp/basket/puck-prep/pre-infusion/pressure-profile/TDS/EY, pressure **machine-conditional**); pour-over (bloom, **pour schedule list**, agitation, drawdown, filter, TDS/EY — supports Hoffmann/Tetsu 4:6 where pours are the lever); immersion (steep/agitation/settle/plunge, AeroPress standard/inverted); stovetop (heat ramp, stop/quench — **deliberately omit bloom/pour**, Hoffmann "grind, heat, stop"). Hybrid brewers (Hario Switch) get a `brew_mode` select + pour list [variables findings; coffeeadastra; hybrid findings]. *Mobile note:* the pour-schedule list is fiddly mid-brew — pair it with X7's timer (tap-to-capture pour timestamps) and `inputMode="decimal"` numeric keypads rather than a raw multi-row form. **Effort M–L** (one family at a time). EY auto-computed: `EY = (C·B)/(D·(1−C))` [coffeeadastra].

**X5. Structured tasting — fully behind "Add detail."** *Correction vs draft:* the draft put a sour↔bitter slider on the **Simple** tier, violating its own Guardrail #1 (a slider next to stars next to free-notes next to would-brew-again = three overlapping "how was it?"). Resolution: **the entire structured-taste layer lives behind "Add detail"; the Simple tier keeps only the existing star row + notes.** *Enthusiast:* sour↔bitter, then sweetness/body/acidity/finish, then an optional SCA flavor-wheel chip picker → radar in trends [foundry-coffee; climpson; kunjani]. *Schema mapping:* `details.taste = {balance:-2..2, sweetness?, body?, acidity?, finish?, descriptors?[]}`; feeds the dial-in coach (Y3). **Effort M.**

**X6. User-saved recipes (table).** Save any brew as a recipe, fork it, set a **per-bean baseline** ("espresso for the Ethiopia") so dialing a new bag starts from the last keeper; a `keeper` flag marks known-good cups. (The *static* starter recipes already shipped in N6.) *Why:* "ship recipe presets users load and tweak — beginners log against a known recipe, enthusiasts fork it" [variables implications; grinder implications; progression]. See Deep-dive table below for schema. **Effort M.**

**X7. In-form brew timer.** A start/stop timer that fills `time_s` (and can capture bloom/pour timestamps for X4). *Why:* `time_s` is core for nearly every method, yet the draft assumed users read it off an external timer and type it. For a beginner-first coffee app, an in-form timer is **more on-brand and higher-leverage than custom fields or reorderable fields** (both demoted to LATER). *Schema mapping:* UI state writes `time_s` (column) and optional `details.events[]` timestamps. **Effort S–M.** *(New; promoted above the old X7/Y4.)*

**X8. Water tracking.** See Deep-dive 3. Categorical by default (Tap/Filtered/Bottled/Distilled+pack/DIY), numeric TDS/GH/KH on demand, stored as a reusable profile. *Why:* "beginner default a categorical picker; power-user numeric; treat water as a saved profile, not per-brew entry; never gate the happy path behind chemistry" [scales implications]. **Effort M.**

**X9. Brew photo + bag-depletion.** Beans already have `photo_url`; brews don't. A cup/crema/pour photo is **more emotionally sticky for a 20s–40s audience than TDS%**. Pair with lightweight bag-depletion ("you've brewed ~14 of 250g") computed from summed `dose_g` per bean — a habit-forming reason to open the app. *Schema mapping:* `alter table brews add column photo_url text` (reuse the bean-photos bucket); depletion is a read-only `sum(dose_g)` query, no schema for the estimate. **Effort S–M.** *(New; from critique's missing-features list.)*

---

### TIER 3 — LATER (payoff, polish, portability — and the enthusiast-only items)

| ID | Feature | Effort | New table? | Touches |
|----|---------|--------|-----------|---------|
| Y1 | **Dashboard & trends** | L | no | queries, trends page |
| Y2 | **Import / export** | M (export) · L (import) | no | export route, import action |
| Y3 | **Guided dial-in suggestions** (crude version earlier) | S–M | no | coach.ts, brew detail |
| Y4 | **Tags** (standalone `text[]` + chip input) | S | no (tags col) | brews.tags, TagInput |
| Y5 | **Onboarding** (1–2 questions → seeded first brew) | M | no | onboarding page (needs N5 + N6) |
| Y6 | **User-defined custom fields** (typed) | M | no (JSONB) | profiles.custom_fields |
| Y7 | **Reorderable / hideable fields** | M | no | profiles.field_layout |
| Y8 | **Soft-delete / undo for brews** | S | no (`deleted_at` col) | actions, queries |

**Y1. Dashboard & trends.** Per-bean "last 5 brews" timeline with rating + the one thing changed each time; an **auto extraction-quality label** ("Slightly under · try finer") from ratio/time; a gentle monthly review (the single most-cited reason long-term journalers continue) [features; progression]. Enthusiast: trend lines, repeatability, "which gear/water correlates with higher ratings" (enabled because gear/water are referenced entities), tasting radar. *Schema mapping:* reads only; extend `queries.ts`; inline-SVG sparklines (no chart dep). **Effort L.**

**Y2. Import / export.** *Correction vs draft:* **export** (RLS-scoped select → CSV/JSON via a Route Handler) is **M**; **import** (FormData CSV with encoding/dup/partial-failure/RLS handling) is the harder half and is **L**, not "M with no schema change." Export closes the lock-in objection that loses power users to Notion/Beanconqueror [features findings]. **Effort M + L.**

**Y3. Guided dial-in.** A friendly one-line next-step on save ("Sour usually means under-extracted — try one click finer. Change one thing 🙂"), method-aware (for 4:6, "more pours = stronger" not grind) and **grinder-aware** (never suggest grinding finer to a pre-ground user, per X3). *Note:* a **crude version** using just the sour/bitter signal + structured change is S and very on-brand — ship it right after X5 if capacity allows, rather than waiting for full trends. *Schema mapping:* `lib/journal/coach.ts`, no schema. **Effort S–M.**

**Y4. Tags.** A `brews.tags text[] not null default '{}'` column + chip input (`#travel`, `#decaf`) for slicing later [features findings]. Split out from the old "custom fields + tags" item because tags are cheap and broadly useful, whereas typed custom fields are not (Y6). **Effort S.**

**Y5. Onboarding.** Two taps (main method + units) → writes N5 prefs → drops into a seeded N6 recipe; checklists lift activation [useronboard; progression]. Depends on N5 + N6. **Effort M.**

**Y6. User-defined custom fields (typed).** *Demoted from the draft's NEXT to LATER.* Pure power-user catnip and the **riskiest server-side validation** in the whole plan: you cannot validate user-defined typed values without `createBrew` fetching `profiles.custom_fields` on every write (an unacknowledged round-trip + coupling). Build it **last**, after trends (Y1) reveal which fields people actually want — many of those should become real promoted columns, not custom fields. *Schema mapping:* definitions in `profiles.custom_fields jsonb`; values in `details.custom.<id>` (no per-field migration). **Effort M.**

**Y7. Reorderable / hideable fields.** Beanconqueror's actual differentiator is *configurability*, not field count [progression findings]. *Schema mapping:* `profiles.field_layout jsonb` keyed by method; the N1 renderer sorts/filters by this overlay. Advanced-only. **Effort M.**

**Y8. Soft-delete / undo.** With richer per-brew data, an accidental delete costs more. Add `deleted_at` and an undo toast rather than hard `delete`. Beginners benefit from forgiveness most. **Effort S.**

---

## Deep-dives (highest-leverage features)

### Deep-dive 1 — Gear profiles (X1): reusable entities replacing freeform `gear_used`

**Beginner UX.** First brew: the Gear field is still basically free — "What did you use?" with a text fallback, so zero gear records still works. Once they add a grinder/brewer once (or pick from the library, X2), it's a tiny dropdown ("Timemore C3 · V60"). Most beginners have one grinder + one or two brewers.
**Enthusiast UX.** An equipment shelf at `/account/journal/gear`: multiple grinders/brewers/scales/machines. Brews reference them by ID → unlocks per-grinder grind widgets (X3) and "do my Niche brews score higher?" analytics (Y1). **Historical brews keep the gear they were made on** through the hand → electric → single-dose upgrade arc [grinder implications].

```sql
-- 0004_gear.sql
create table public.gear (
  id          uuid primary key default gen_random_uuid(),
  user_id     uuid not null references auth.users(id) on delete cascade,
  kind        text not null check (kind in ('grinder','brewer','machine','scale','kettle')),
  name        text not null,                       -- "Timemore C3"
  brand       text,
  model       text,
  spec        jsonb not null default '{}'::jsonb,  -- grinder adjustment / brewer geometry (X3)
  library_id  text,                                -- nullable ref to built-in library (X2)
  archived_at timestamptz,
  created_at  timestamptz not null default now(),
  updated_at  timestamptz not null default now()
);
create index gear_user_idx on public.gear (user_id, kind, archived_at);
alter table public.gear enable row level security;

create policy "gear: read own"   on public.gear for select using ( auth.uid() = user_id );
create policy "gear: insert own" on public.gear for insert with check ( auth.uid() = user_id );
create policy "gear: update own" on public.gear for update
  using ( auth.uid() = user_id ) with check ( auth.uid() = user_id );  -- see note
create policy "gear: delete own" on public.gear for delete using ( auth.uid() = user_id );
```
- `brews`: add `grinder_id uuid references gear(id) on delete set null` and `brewer_id uuid references gear(id) on delete set null`; keep `gear_used text` for legacy/free entries (history preserved exactly like `bean_id`).
- New `GearForm.tsx`, `GearPicker.tsx` (dropdown + inline "add new," mirroring the existing "+ New bean" pattern); `createGear`/`updateGear`/`archiveGear` copy the bean action shape (same `requireUserId`).

> **RLS correction (from critique, verified).** The existing `beans`/`brews` UPDATE policies use `using(...)` with **no `WITH CHECK`** (0001 lines 80–81, 140–142). Copying that "verbatim" would let an UPDATE reassign a row's `user_id` to someone else. New tables therefore add `with check ( auth.uid() = user_id )` on UPDATE (shown above), and a small follow-up migration should backfill `WITH CHECK` onto `beans`/`brews` too. Server actions never set `user_id` on update, so risk is low — but don't propagate the loose pattern.

### Deep-dive 2 — Per-method fields (N1): five stable slots, relabeled

The Simple form is **always five slots**; the method changes their *labels*, not their *count*:

| Slot | Espresso | Pour-over | Immersion (AeroPress / French press) | Stovetop (moka) | Drip-bag |
|------|----------|-----------|--------------------------------------|-----------------|----------|
| Dose | Dose (g) | Dose (g) | Dose (g) | Dose (g) | Dose (g, pre-filled) |
| Liquid | **Yield (g)** | **Water (g) / ratio** | **Water (g)** | **Water (g)** | **Water (g)** |
| Time | **Shot time (s)** | Brew time | **Steep time** | Brew time | Brew time |
| Grind | Grind | Grind | Grind | Grind | **— (pre-ground)** |
| Taste | Rating + notes | Rating + notes | Rating + notes | Rating + notes | Rating + notes |

A beginner never sees a field that doesn't apply (no shot-time on a French press), **and the screen never changes shape** between sessions — it relabels in place. Advanced (N2) then reveals family-appropriate depth (X4) without ever touching the Simple five. *Why the relabel-not-remove rule matters:* the draft's "fewer cards = pure win" ignored the consistency cost for entry users; stable layout is itself a beginner feature [variables-per-method implications; mooremomentum].

**Implementation reality (verified):** `MethodPicker` is uncontrolled, so N1a must make it controlled before N1b's config can drive re-renders. `BrewForm`'s current hardcoded `FIELDS`/`NumField` block (lines 22–29, 105–143) becomes `fields.filter(f => f.depth==='core').map(renderField)`, keeping `NumField`/`TextField` and adding `GrindField`/`RatioField`/`BloomField`/`PoursField`/`SelectField`/`TimerField` as `kind` handlers.

### Deep-dive 3 — Water tracking (X8): categorical by default, chemistry on demand

**Beginner UX.** A single categorical picker (inside Advanced, or a quiet optional core slot): **Tap · Filtered · Bottled · Distilled+pack · DIY.** The happy path stays fully usable logging only beans + dose + water + time — never gated behind chemistry.
**Enthusiast UX.** Define a Water Profile once (name + TDS ppm + GH + KH + recipe text like "Third Wave Classic" / "rpavlis"), attach by dropdown, with gentle SCA hints (TDS ~150 target, GH:KH ~2:1) [scales findings]. Serious brewers hold water constant for months, so a saved profile beats per-brew entry.

```sql
-- 0006_water.sql
create table public.water_profiles (
  id            uuid primary key default gen_random_uuid(),
  user_id       uuid not null references auth.users(id) on delete cascade,
  name          text not null,
  kind          text,            -- 'tap'|'filtered'|'bottled'|'distilled+pack'|'diy'
  tds_ppm       int, gh_ppm int, kh_ppm int,
  recipe_notes  text,
  created_at    timestamptz not null default now(),
  updated_at    timestamptz not null default now()
);
alter table public.water_profiles enable row level security;
-- four policies, auth.uid() = user_id, WITH CHECK on insert AND update (see RLS note)
```
- `brews`: add `water_id uuid references water_profiles(id) on delete set null`; the quick categorical can also live in `details.water`. **Schema is ready for graduation with no migration — but chemistry never shows unless asked.**

### Deep-dive 4 — Profile preferences (N5): defaults, per-family mode, and safe units

```sql
-- 0003_profile_prefs.sql
alter table public.profiles
  add column mass_unit       text not null default 'g'   check (mass_unit in ('g','oz')),
  add column temp_unit       text not null default 'c'   check (temp_unit in ('c','f')),
  add column default_method  public.brew_method,
  add column brew_detail     jsonb not null default '{}'::jsonb,  -- per-family Simple/Advanced
  add column default_gear_id uuid;  -- FK added in X1 once gear exists
```
- `brew_detail` is **per method-family** (e.g. `{"espresso":"advanced"}`), implementing N2's correction.
- New `getProfilePrefs()` in `queries.ts`; `updatePrefs` server action; `lib/journal/units.ts` (`toDisplay`/`fromDisplay`); storage stays canonical (grams, °C) — convert only at the display edge.

> **Unit-rounding correction (from critique, verified).** `water_temp_c` and `time_s` are **integer** columns (0001 lines 112/114). The draft's "client converts to canonical on submit" silently corrupts: a US user typing 200 °F → 93.33 °C → stored `93` → displayed `199 °F`. Off-by-one temps erode trust for exactly the beginner who can't tell app from grinder. **Before shipping oz/°F display:** (1) migrate `water_temp_c` to `numeric(4,1)` (and treat dose/yield as the already-numeric columns they are), (2) apply explicit **half-up** rounding only at the display edge, and (3) round-trip-test the F↔C and oz↔g paths so the number a beginner typed is the number they see back.

---

## Guardrails — what NOT to do

Firm. Reviewers should reject changes that violate these.

1. **Never add an always-on field to the Simple form.** Simple is capped at the **five stable slots**; each new field defaults to Advanced or is gear/method-conditional. (This is why X5's taste layer moved entirely behind "Add detail" — the draft's simple-tier slider violated this rule.) [mooremomentum]
2. **Never change the Simple form's *shape* between methods.** Relabel slots; never add/remove cards. Predictability is a beginner feature. [variables-per-method implications]
3. **Make the brand mechanic reachable on brew #1.** "Change one thing" must teach even with no prior brew to repeat — never let it be visible only when pre-filling.
4. **Never block the happy path behind gear, water, or chemistry.** Fully usable logging only bean + dose + water/yield + time + rating. No required grinder profile, water, or TDS. [scales implications; progression]
5. **Never show enthusiast jargon to a beginner.** No "EY%", "GH:KH", "pre-infusion bar", "µm/click" outside Advanced. Friendly, no-gatekeeping voice. [foundry-coffee; brand voice]
6. **Never compute grind size naively.** No `setting × constant`; scales are non-linear (Encore ESP 20µm then 90µm). Store raw setting tied to the grinder; microns optional/derived. **And: allow "pre-ground / can't change" as a grind value, and never tell a pre-ground user to grind finer.** [grinder implications]
7. **Don't require a refractometer, kettle, or scale.** EY/TDS optional everywhere; don't force kettle/scale fields on Clever/French press. [variables; scales implications]
8. **No forced data migrations on graduation.** Depth layers alongside flat columns (entities + one JSONB). Old `grind_clicks`/`gear_used` keep meaning forever (`on delete set null`, never cascade-clobber). [grinder implications]
9. **Respect RLS on every new table — including `WITH CHECK` on UPDATE.** Same four `auth.uid() = user_id` policies as `beans`, **plus** the `WITH CHECK` the originals omit. Every action calls `requireUserId()`. (verified gap in 0001)
10. **Validate `details` server-side against the method config.** Coerce by `kind`, drop unknown keys, clamp per-family (espresso 1:2 vs filter 1:16). For typed custom fields (Y6), `createBrew` must fetch `profiles.custom_fields` to validate — build that last. [variables/scales implications]
11. **Guard the units math.** Canonical storage; half-up rounding at the edge only; widen integer temp to numeric before shipping °F/oz. Don't let unit display corrupt the numbers beginners are learning from. (verified integer columns)
12. **Mobile-first input.** `inputMode="decimal"` keypads, large touch targets, and tap-to-capture timing (X7) for pour schedules — never a raw multi-row form mid-brew.
13. **Don't make customization a chore, and don't over-rely on streaks.** Custom fields, reordering, water, recipes are opt-in accelerators; if a feature slows the first brew, it's mis-tiered. Frame consistency as "more days than not," allow back-dating. [progression]

---

## If you only ship 3 things this month

1. **N1a + N1b — config-driven, per-method form (with the controlled-MethodPicker refactor first).** This is the structural unlock for everything else and the fix for the "one flat form for every method" problem. Budget for the refactor as the real work, not the config. Ship espresso's field set first, keeping the five stable slots for Simple.
2. **N6 + N3 cold-start — seeded starter recipes *and* "change one thing" from brew #1.** Together these make the *first* brew on-brand: a first-brewer lands on a known-good recipe instead of a blank form, and meets Nudo's defining mechanic immediately. For a *"For every first brews"* brand, the empty state is the front door — this is the highest brand-leverage pair in the whole plan.
3. **N0 + N5 — edit-brew action + preferences/units (with the rounding/temp-column fix).** `updateBrew` is a genuine quick win and the prerequisite for trusting any richer data; preferences make defaults and per-family Advanced mode work. Do the integer→numeric temp migration and half-up rounding *as part of* N5 so units never corrupt beginners' numbers.

*(Deliberately deferred: gear entities/water/timer are the right NEXT, but they depend on N1's config + preferences landing first. User-defined custom fields and reorderable fields are LATER — lowest beginner value, highest validation risk.)*
